issues.original: I have shipped four agent systems on Alibaba Cloud in the last eighteen months. Three of them started life as a [CODE] session on a single ECS instance someone created by clicking through the console. All three of those needed a panicked weekend of rebuilding when the second engineer joined the project, when the prod region had a stockout, or when the security team asked for a network diagram.
issues.fixed: In the last eighteen months, I've shipped four agent systems on Alibaba Cloud. Three began as a [CODE] session on a single ECS instance created by clicking through the console. Each required a frantic weekend of rebuilding when a second engineer joined, when the prod region had a stockout, or when the security team asked for a network diagram.

issues.original: The fourth started life as [CODE]. It was the only one I haven't lost a weekend to.
issues.fixed: The fourth started as [CODE], and it's the only one that didn't cost me a weekend.

issues.original: This series is the field guide for that fourth pattern: how to use Terraform to provision the cloud infrastructure that an AI agent system actually needs on Alibaba Cloud. It is not a Terraform tutorial — there are good ones online and the official [CODE] doc covers the basics. It is the senior-engineer playbook for the specific intersection of 'I run agents' and 'I run them on Aliyun'.
issues.fixed: This series is a field guide for the fourth pattern: using Terraform to provision the cloud infrastructure an AI agent system needs on Alibaba Cloud. It's not a Terraform tutorial—good ones are available online, and the official [CODE] doc covers the basics. This is the senior-engineer playbook for running agents on Aliyun.

issues.original: Before we talk infrastructure, let's name the components an agent system has — the ones a [CODE] README usually skips:
issues.fixed: Before discussing infrastructure, let's list the components an agent system typically includes—those often omitted in a [CODE] README:

issues.original: That is at least nine separate Aliyun services touching each other in specific ways. Each has its own console page, its own RAM permissions, its own region scoping, its own networking. The probability that you can wire all of this up by hand and have it still match across [CODE], [CODE], and [CODE] after three months of evolution is roughly zero.
issues.fixed: These are at least nine separate Aliyun services, each with its own console page, RAM permissions, region scoping, and networking. Manually setting this up and keeping it consistent across [CODE], [CODE], and [CODE] over three months is nearly impossible.

issues.original: Nine services managed manually create nine drift surfaces. This pain pattern is so common that I have a standard figure for it:
issues.fixed: Managing nine services manually creates nine drift surfaces. This issue is so common that I have a standard figure for it:

issues.original: Read the left column carefully. Every step is plausible — none of them are dumb mistakes. They are what happens when smart people make small reasonable decisions over months. The right column is the same path, but every step leaves an artifact in git. The diff between the two columns is the difference between 'I shipped this' and 'I am paged at 2am because nobody knows what's running in [CODE].'
issues.fixed: Carefully read the left column. Each step is plausible—no dumb mistakes here. These are the results of smart people making small, reasonable decisions over time. The right column shows the same path, but each step leaves an artifact in Git. The difference between the two columns is the difference between 'I shipped this' and 'I'm paged at 2 AM because no one knows what's running in [CODE].'

issues.original: Terraform is an open-source declarative tool from HashiCorp. You write [CODE] files in **HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL)** that describe the cloud resources you want; Terraform diffs that desired state against the live state recorded in a **state file** and emits a **plan**; you review the plan; you [CODE] it; Terraform translates the plan into provider API calls.
issues.fixed: Terraform is an open-source declarative tool from HashiCorp. You write [CODE] files in **HashiCorp Configuration Language (HCL)** to describe the cloud resources you want. Terraform compares the desired state with the live state in a **state file** and generates a **plan**. You review the plan, apply it, and Terraform translates the plan into provider API calls.

issues.original: Three key points to remember: - **Declarative, not imperative.** You don't say 'create an instance' — you say 'an instance of this shape exists.' Re-running the same config is a no-op if nothing changed. This is what makes Terraform safe to run from CI on every commit. - **State is real.** The [CODE] file is a JSON map from your HCL resource addresses to the cloud's actual resource IDs. Lose the state file and Terraform thinks nothing exists. Article 2 is about putting state somewhere durable — but the implications run deeper than 'don't lose the file,' and we'll come back to that below. - **Plan before apply.** This is the killer feature. Every change shows you a literal diff of what will create, modify, or destroy *before* anything happens. Cultivate the habit of pasting the plan output into PR descriptions — your future self will thank you.
issues.fixed: Three key points to remember: - **Declarative, not imperative.** You say 'an instance of this shape exists' instead of 'create an instance.' Re-running the same config is a no-op if nothing changed, making Terraform safe to run from CI on every commit. - **State is real.** The [CODE] file maps HCL resource addresses to the cloud's actual resource IDs. Losing the state file means Terraform thinks nothing exists. Article 2 covers storing state durably, but the implications go beyond just not losing the file. - **Plan before apply.** This is the killer feature. Every change shows a literal diff of what will create, modify, or destroy *before* anything happens. Paste the plan output into PR descriptions—your future self will thank you.

issues.original: The 'state is real' point deserves more than one bullet, because for an agent stack the state file is doing double duty as your inventory. Every agent stack I've shipped has been audited at some point—by a security review, by finance reconciling cloud spend, or by a new SRE trying to figure out what's running. The question is always the same: *what exists, who created it, and what is it costing me?* If your infrastructure is in a Terraform state file, that question takes 30 seconds to answer: For the four agent stacks I run today, those three commands produce a comprehensive inventory in seconds. Before Terraform, the same audit required opening twelve console tabs across ECS, VPC, RDS, OSS, RAM, KMS, SLS, ARMS, ACK, CloudMonitor, ALB, and OpenSearch—filtered by tags if I was lucky, and by gut feeling if I wasn't. The state file is also a *bill of materials* in the supply-chain sense. Each resource carries its provider version and module source. When a CVE drops on the alicloud provider — and it does, a couple of times a year — you grep state files across all your projects in minutes: The deeper point: **state turns infrastructure into data**. Once it's data, you can write tooling against it. I have a small Python scr
issues.fixed: The 'state is real' point deserves more than one bullet because, for an agent stack, the state file serves as both state and inventory. Every agent stack I've shipped has been audited—by security, finance, or a new SRE. The question is always the same: *what exists, who created it, and what is it costing me?* With a Terraform state file, answering this takes 30 seconds. For the four agent stacks I run, these three commands produce a comprehensive inventory in seconds. Before Terraform, the same audit required opening twelve console tabs across ECS, VPC, RDS, OSS, RAM, KMS, SLS, ARMS, ACK, CloudMonitor, ALB, and OpenSearch—filtered by tags if I was lucky, and by gut feeling if I wasn't. The state file is also a *bill of materials* in the supply-chain sense. Each resource includes its provider version and module source. When a CVE hits the alicloud provider—and it does a couple of times a year—you can grep state files across all your projects in minutes. The deeper point: **state turns infrastructure into data**. Once it's data, you can write tooling against it. I have a small Python script

issues.original: I won't dwell — the official [CODE] doc covers all OSes. On macOS:
issues.fixed: I won't dwell — the official [CODE] doc covers all OSes. For macOS:

issues.original: Pinning early is cheap insurance. The alicloud provider has introduced breaking changes between minor versions (the OSS bucket schema rework around 1.220 cost me three afternoons). You will eventually need to upgrade — do it deliberately in a PR, with the diff in plan output, not by accident on a teammate's laptop at 11pm.
issues.fixed: Pinning early is cheap insurance. The alicloud provider has introduced breaking changes between minor versions (the OSS bucket schema rework around 1.220 cost me three afternoons). When you need to upgrade, do it deliberately in a PR, with the diff in plan output, not by accident on a teammate's laptop at 11pm.

issues.original: The role has the actual write permissions; the AK only has the right to assume it. STS sessions are short-lived (one hour by default), audit-logged in ActionTrail, and can be revoked instantly by detaching the trust policy. This is the model GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, and Jenkins runners should use.
issues.fixed: The role has the actual write permissions; the AK only has the right to assume it. STS sessions are short-lived (one hour by default), audit-logged in ActionTrail, and can be revoked instantly by detaching the trust policy. This is the recommended model for GitLab CI, GitHub Actions, and Jenkins runners.

issues.original: If [CODE] runs on an Aliyun ECS instance — your team's ops bastion or the Aliyun-hosted IaC Service runner — attach a RAM role to the instance and the provider picks credentials up automatically from instance metadata:
issues.fixed: If [CODE] runs on an Aliyun ECS instance (like your team's ops bastion or the Aliyun-hosted IaC Service runner), attach a RAM role to the instance. The provider will pick up credentials automatically from instance metadata:

issues.original: That file is the source of truth for what infrastructure exists. Three things will go wrong:
issues.fixed: That file is the source of truth for your infrastructure. Three things can go wrong:

issues.original: A common misconception: tfstate just stores resource IDs. False. The state file contains every attribute Terraform knows about every resource — including computed ones like RDS connection strings, generated passwords, KMS key material, and [CODE] variables.
issues.fixed: A common misconception is that tfstate only stores resource IDs. In fact, the state file contains every attribute Terraform knows about each resource, including computed ones like RDS connection strings, generated passwords, KMS key material, and [CODE] variables.

issues.original: The OSS bucket holding state must be KMS-encrypted. We will turn this on in step 4. If you skip it, anyone with [CODE] on the bucket reads your secrets.
issues.fixed: The OSS bucket holding the state must be KMS-encrypted. We'll enable this in step 4. If you skip it, anyone with [CODE] on the bucket can read your secrets.

issues.original: This article builds the single most copied piece of Terraform in my agent projects: a [CODE] module that gives every later component (ECS, RDS, OpenSearch, ACK) a sane place to land. It's about 200 lines of HCL all-in. Worth typing once, refer to it forever.
issues.fixed: This article builds the most copied piece of Terraform in my agent projects: a [CODE] module that provides a sensible landing place for each subsequent component (ECS, RDS, OpenSearch, ACK). It's about 200 lines of HCL. Type it once and refer to it forever.

issues.original: Before code, the picture: Why three zones? Because Aliyun reserves the right to do a zone-level maintenance on any given Sunday, and a single-zone deployment means your agents are offline for the whole window. Cross-zone traffic inside a VPC is free; the only cost of three zones is the operational complexity of subnet math, which Terraform absorbs for you.
issues.fixed: Before the code, here's the picture: Why three zones? Aliyun can perform zone-level maintenance on any given Sunday, and a single-zone deployment means your agents are offline for the entire window. Cross-zone traffic inside a VPC is free. The only cost of using three zones is the operational complexity of subnet math, which Terraform handles for you.

issues.original: Why public + private? The agent runtime should live in private vSwitches so a misconfigured security group can't accidentally expose it on [CODE]. Public vSwitches hold the ALB and the NAT Gateway — things that *must* reach the internet. The agent reaches the internet via NAT, never directly.
issues.fixed: Why public and private? The agent runtime should be in private vSwitches to prevent a misconfigured security group from accidentally exposing it. Public vSwitches host the ALB and the NAT Gateway, which must reach the internet. The agent accesses the internet via NAT, never directly.

issues.original: Requiring exactly three zones is opinionated but aligns with the diagram. If you need two or four zones, fork the module—don't make it conditional. Conditional modules become unreadable and often get rewritten from scratch six months later.
issues.fixed: Requiring exactly three zones is opinionated but aligns with the diagram. If you need two or four zones, fork the module—don't make it conditional. Conditional modules become unreadable and often need to be rewritten from scratch six months later.

issues.original: The SNAT entries are what actually let private-subnet instances reach the internet. Without them, an agent in [CODE] cannot resolve [CODE] — and you will spend an hour debugging that the first time it bites you. I have the scar.
issues.fixed: The SNAT entries allow private-subnet instances to reach the internet. Without them, an agent in [CODE] cannot resolve [CODE], and you'll spend an hour debugging the first time it happens. Trust me, I've been there.

issues.original: By the time you're done, attaching an ECS to the right SG is just [CODE] and the network tier is correct by construction. Audit becomes trivial: you grep for the SG name and find every resource that ever held it.
issues.fixed: By the time you're done, attaching an ECS to the right SG is as simple as [CODE], and the network tier is correct by construction. Auditing becomes trivial: you can grep for the SG name and find every resource that ever used it.

issues.original: Why the alias? Because the CMK ID is a UUID nobody remembers; the alias [CODE] is human-readable and stable across key rotations. Reference the alias from RDS, OSS, and SLS, and you can swap the underlying key without touching downstream config.
issues.fixed: Why the alias? The CMK ID is a UUID that no one remembers, while the alias [CODE] is human-readable and stable across key rotations. Referencing the alias from RDS, OSS, and SLS allows you to swap the underlying key without changing downstream config.

issues.original: An agent's memory is the part most tutorials gloss over. "Just put the embeddings in Pinecone, the sessions in Postgres, and the screenshots in S3." On Aliyun, all three are managed services, and provisioning them correctly with Terraform can mean the difference between a working memory and losing three weeks of conversation history because the disk filled up at 4 AM.
issues.fixed: Most tutorials gloss over an agent's memory. 'Just put the embeddings in Pinecone, the sessions in Postgres, and the screenshots in S3.' On Aliyun, all three are managed services. Correctly provisioning them with Terraform can mean the difference between a working memory and losing three weeks of conversation history because the disk filled up at 4 AM.

issues.original: This article covers all three layers, their Terraform configurations, the critical but tedious backup and disaster recovery (DR) setup, the major version upgrade process, and the Saturday outage that taught me everything I now do differently.
issues.fixed: This article covers all three layers, their Terraform configurations, the critical but tedious backup and disaster recovery (DR) setup, the major version upgrade process, and the Saturday outage that changed how I do things.

issues.original: Don't conflate them. I once watched a team try to put 50 GB of generated PDFs in Postgres because 'it has a [CODE] column'. Cost ten times what OSS would have, query latency went to mush, backups took hours. Each layer has a service that's good at exactly its job — pick the right one and the bill stays sane.
issues.fixed: Don't conflate them. I once saw a team try to store 50 GB of generated PDFs in Postgres because 'it has a [CODE] column.' It cost ten times more than OSS, query latency suffered, and backups took hours. Each layer has a service that's good at its specific job — pick the right one to keep costs reasonable.

issues.original: For session state—turn-by-turn conversation, tool call traces, and user identity—you need a robust RDBMS. PostgreSQL is my go-to, but MySQL is fine if your team prefers it. PolarDB is the next step up when you need horizontal scaling.
issues.fixed: For session state—turn-by-turn conversation, tool call traces, and user identity—you need a robust RDBMS. PostgreSQL is my go-to, but MySQL is fine if your team prefers it. Use PolarDB when you need horizontal scaling.

issues.original: A small relational layer is the backbone. The next layer is what makes the agent feel intelligent.
issues.fixed: A small relational layer is the backbone. The next layer makes the agent feel intelligent.

issues.original: The cost is real (~¥800/mo for the smallest instance) but you get hybrid lexical+vector search out of the box, which is the right shape for retrieval — pure vector similarity loses to BM25 on too many real queries to ignore.
issues.fixed: The cost is real (~¥800/mo for the smallest instance), but you get hybrid lexical+vector search, which is better for retrieval. Pure vector similarity often loses to BM25 on real queries.

issues.original: OSS is where artifacts go: generated images, PDFs, screenshots, run-trace tarballs, model checkpoints if you fine-tune. For an agent stack:
issues.fixed: OSS is for artifacts: generated images, PDFs, screenshots, run-trace tarballs, and model checkpoints if you fine-tune. For an agent stack:

issues.original: For agent artifacts, the rule is: keep 30 days in Standard, two months in Infrequent Access, nine months in Archive, and one year in Cold Archive, then delete. For a 1 TB artifact corpus, this means the difference between ~¥1500/mo (all Standard) and ~¥250/mo. Codify this in HCL once to save significantly over a year. The only pitfalls are the 30-day minimum storage charge for IA and the Archive retrieval latency—avoid putting hot data in cold tiers.
issues.fixed: For agent artifacts, keep 30 days in Standard, two months in Infrequent Access, nine months in Archive, and one year in Cold Archive, then delete. For a 1 TB artifact corpus, this means the difference between ~¥1500/mo (all Standard) and ~¥250/mo. Codify this in HCL to save significantly over a year. The main pitfalls are the 30-day minimum storage charge for IA and the Archive retrieval latency—avoid putting hot data in cold tiers.

issues.original: A pattern I see repeatedly in immature agent stacks: each agent has its own copy of [CODE] in its own [CODE] file. Sometimes the same key, sometimes different ones, sometimes a colleague's personal key from when they prototyped. When the bill arrives nobody can tell which agent caused which token spend, and when a key leaks (it always does) you're playing whack-a-mole across a dozen [CODE] files.
issues.fixed: In immature agent stacks, each agent often has its own copy of [CODE] in its own [CODE] file. Sometimes it's the same key, sometimes different ones, or even a colleague's personal key from prototyping. When the bill comes, no one can tell which agent caused which token spend. And when a key leaks (which it always does), you end up chasing down leaks across a dozen [CODE] files.

issues.original: The real wake-up call hit me two years ago. A contractor finished his three-month engagement on a Friday, his laptop went home, and on the following Tuesday DashScope billing flagged 12 million tokens of [CODE] traffic from an IP we didn't recognise. His personal API key — copy-pasted into a side project — was still sitting in our agent's [CODE]. Rotating it took six hours: three engineers, four repos, two CI pipelines, one panicked Slack thread. Never again.
issues.fixed: The real wake-up call came two years ago. A contractor finished his three-month engagement on a Friday, and on the following Tuesday, DashScope billing flagged 12 million tokens of [CODE] traffic from an unrecognized IP. His personal API key, copy-pasted into a side project, was still in our agent's [CODE]. It took six hours to rotate it: three engineers, four repos, two CI pipelines, and one panicked Slack thread. Never again.

issues.original: This article addresses that pattern. We build one **LLM gateway** that:
issues.fixed: This article addresses that pattern by building one **LLM gateway** that:

issues.original: You have two reasonable options:
issues.fixed: You have two options:

issues.original: I use both, depending on routing complexity. For a simple proxy with quotas, API Gateway is enough. For multi-provider routing with budget guards and circuit breakers — which most teams need within six months — LiteLLM on ECS is better. The rest of this article focuses on LiteLLM because it suits 80% of teams.
issues.fixed: I use both, depending on the routing complexity. For a simple proxy with quotas, API Gateway is sufficient. For multi-provider routing with budget guards and circuit breakers — which most teams need within six months — LiteLLM on ECS is better. The rest of this article focuses on LiteLLM because it suits 80% of teams.

issues.original: The first rule: provider keys never appear in [CODE] files, in [CODE] blocks, in agent code, or in tfstate plaintext. They live in KMS Secrets Manager and the gateway pulls them at startup via STS.
issues.fixed: The first rule: provider keys should never appear in [CODE] files, [CODE] blocks, agent code, or tfstate plaintext. They live in KMS Secrets Manager, and the gateway pulls them at startup via STS.

issues.original: Cost note: KMS Secrets Manager bills around ¥0.4 per secret per month plus ¥0.03 per 10k API calls in Shanghai region. For four provider keys pulled twice an hour by two gateway boxes, the monthly bill is rounding error — under ¥10. The default service KMS key is free; only customer-managed CMKs cost ¥1/month each. Don't let 'KMS sounds expensive' be the reason you stay on [CODE] files.
issues.fixed: Cost note: KMS Secrets Manager charges about ¥0.4 per secret per month plus ¥0.03 per 10k API calls in the Shanghai region. For four provider keys pulled twice an hour by two gateway boxes, the monthly bill is negligible — under ¥10. The default service KMS key is free; only customer-managed CMKs cost ¥1/month each. Don't let 'KMS sounds expensive' keep you on [CODE] files.

issues.original: Three key points here:
issues.fixed: Three key points:

issues.original: LiteLLM is the easiest open-source LLM proxy I know. It uses the OpenAI API format on the frontend and translates to each provider's format on the backend. Self-hosting it on ECS keeps things flexible:
issues.fixed: LiteLLM is the easiest open-source LLM proxy I know. It uses the OpenAI API format on the frontend and translates to each provider's format on the backend. Self-hosting it on ECS keeps things flexible.

issues.original: Agents now reach the gateway at [CODE] and never see a provider key. Internal-only ALB — no public IP, no listener on 443 facing the world. If an agent needs to call from outside the VPC, it goes through the bastion or CEN, never directly.
issues.fixed: Agents now reach the gateway at [CODE] and never see a provider key. The ALB is internal-only, with no public IP or listener on 443. If an agent needs to call from outside the VPC, it goes through the bastion or CEN, not directly.

issues.original: Agents are non-deterministic, multi-step, and call expensive APIs. This combination means you can't debug them after the fact unless you instrumented them from the start. This article sets up three pipelines through Terraform — logs, traces, and metrics — into a unified dashboard, adds six SLS queries to solve real incidents, and sets up four alarms that have actually fired and saved my projects in production.
issues.fixed: Agents are non-deterministic, multi-step, and call expensive APIs. This combination means you can't debug them after the fact unless you instrumented them from the start. This article sets up three pipelines through Terraform — logs, traces, and metrics — into a unified dashboard, adds six SLS queries to solve real incidents, and sets up four alarms that have actually fired and saved my projects in production.

issues.original: By the end, you'll have a DingTalk channel that alerts you before the bill explodes, latency increases, the error rate spikes, or an agent starts looping on itself — plus SLO budgets that turn operational feelings into data.
issues.fixed: By the end, you'll have a DingTalk channel that alerts you before the bill explodes, latency increases, the error rate spikes, or an agent starts looping on itself — plus SLO budgets that turn operational feelings into data.

issues.original: The cheapest mistake here is shipping with only [CODE] to stdout. The most expensive is shipping with all three but never opening the dashboard until the first incident — at which point you discover your [CODE] field is sometimes [CODE] because the SDK didn't propagate context. Wire it on day one, then poke it weekly so you know it works.

issues.original: The two env vars come from ARMS — [CODE] is in the ARMS console, [CODE] from your account. Wire both via Terraform outputs into the cloud-init template.
issues.fixed: The two env vars come from ARMS — [CODE] is in the ARMS console, and [CODE] from your account. Wire both via Terraform outputs into the cloud-init template.

issues.original: The reward: in ARMS you can see 'this agent run took 12s; 9s of it was the third LLM call to qwen-max.' That's the kind of visibility that actually changes how you build agents. The first time I saw a span tree from a real production session I rewrote the planner to skip retrieval when the user message was short — saved ~30% on p95 the same week.
issues.fixed: The reward: in ARMS you can see 'this agent run took 12s; 9s of it was the third LLM call to qwen-max.' That's the kind of visibility that actually changes how you build agents. The first time I saw a span tree from a real production session, I rewrote the planner to skip retrieval when the user message was short — saving ~30% on p95 the same week.

issues.original: ## Step 4: metrics with CloudMonitor
issues.fixed: ## Step 4: Metrics with CloudMonitor

issues.original: CloudMonitor catches the host-level metrics automatically (CPU, memory, network) once you install the cloud-monitor agent — which the [CODE] flag on the ACK node pool already does. For ECS, add to cloud-init:
issues.fixed: CloudMonitor catches the host-level metrics automatically (CPU, memory, network) once you install the cloud-monitor agent — which the [CODE] flag on the ACK node pool already does. For ECS, add to cloud-init:

issues.original: For custom application metrics — 'tokens consumed by research-agent' — emit them as SLS log entries with structured fields, then alert via SLS query. SLS-as-metrics is the pattern Aliyun pushes; CloudMonitor custom metrics work too but are clunkier to wire from Terraform, and you end up splitting your alerts across two consoles. Pick one. I pick SLS for everything except host CPU/memory/disk.
issues.fixed: For custom application metrics — 'tokens consumed by research-agent' — emit them as SLS log entries with structured fields, then alert via SLS query. SLS-as-metrics is the pattern Aliyun pushes; CloudMonitor custom metrics work too but are clunkier to wire from Terraform, and you end up splitting your alerts across two consoles. Pick one. I pick SLS for everything except host CPU, memory, and disk.

issues.fixed: ### Query 3: Token-Per-Step Distribution to Find Runaway Loops

issues.original: Sessions with > 50 steps or > 500k tokens are usually loops. The runaway alarm fires; this query identifies the specific session ID. SSH to the box and [CODE] the offender, then post-mortem in calm.

issues.original: This is where everything from articles 2 through 7 lands in one place. By the end you'll have run [CODE] once and produced a complete, observable, budgeted agent runtime stack on Alibaba Cloud — about 31 resources, ~7 minutes of wall clock, ¥12,530/month all-in at prod sizing.

issues.original: The stack we're building: Five layers — edge, compute, memory, platform, ops — composed from the modules we built across this series. Eleven Aliyun products under the hood: VPC, ECS, ALB, OSS, RDS for PostgreSQL, OpenSearch, KMS, SLS, ARMS, CloudMonitor, and DashScope (the LLM provider, accessed via the gateway).

It uses eleven Aliyun products: VPC, ECS, ALB, OSS, RDS for PostgreSQL, OpenSearch, KMS, SLS, ARMS, CloudMonitor, and DashScope (the LLM provider, accessed via the gateway).

issues.original: Eight [CODE] files at the top, five modules in [CODE], environment values in [CODE], secrets out of git in [CODE]. This is the layout I use on every project — boring is good. The thing I will not negotiate on is the [CODE] directory being in [CODE] from commit zero. Every leaked-key incident I've cleaned up traced back to someone adding the gitignore entry on commit 50 instead of commit 1.

issues.original: Five module calls. Each module takes the *previous* module's output as input — [CODE] reads [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]. That dependency wiring is what Terraform uses to build the apply DAG:
issues.fixed: There are five module calls. Each module takes the previous module's output as input — [CODE] reads [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]. This dependency wiring is what Terraform uses to build the apply DAG:

issues.original: VPC and KMS sit at the top with no dependencies. Storage and gateway depend on VPC and KMS but are independent of each other, so Terraform builds them in parallel. Compute depends on all three because the cloud-init template needs their endpoints. Observability resources fan out at the end, referencing SG IDs from compute.
issues.fixed: VPC and KMS sit at the top with no dependencies. Storage and gateway depend on VPC and KMS but are independent of each other, so Terraform builds them in parallel. Compute depends on all three because the cloud-init template needs their endpoints. Observability resources fan out at the end, referencing SG IDs from compute.

issues.original: The [CODE] ternaries are the entire promotion strategy in three lines: prod gets HA RDS, two gateway instances, three agent ECS, ¥800 cost ceiling, cross-region DR. Dev gets the smallest viable shape. Same modules, different sizing, no environment-specific code paths to maintain.
issues.fixed: The [CODE] ternaries encapsulate the entire promotion strategy in three lines: prod gets HA RDS, two gateway instances, three agent ECS, a ¥800 cost ceiling, and cross-region DR. Dev gets the smallest viable shape. Same modules, different sizing, no environment-specific code paths to maintain.

issues.original: Real timing on a fresh apply: The wall-clock breakdown: - **0–60s:** VPC, vSwitch, NAT, EIP, KMS keys — fast resources - **60–380s:** RDS (5 minutes), OpenSearch (5.5 minutes), ECS (~2 minutes), gateway (~1.5 minutes) — all in parallel, gated by the slowest - **380–460s:** agent app deploy via cloud-init, observability resources, alarms
issues.fixed: Real timing on a fresh apply: - **0–60s:** VPC, vSwitch, NAT, EIP, KMS keys — fast resources - **60–380s:** RDS (5 minutes), OpenSearch (5.5 minutes), ECS (~2 minutes), gateway (~1.5 minutes) — all in parallel, gated by the slowest - **380–460s:** agent app deployment via cloud-init, observability resources, and alarms

issues.original: About 7 minutes total, dominated by RDS and OpenSearch provisioning. Re-applies on no-change runs take under 30 seconds because Terraform only diffs.
issues.fixed: Total time is about 7 minutes, dominated by RDS and OpenSearch provisioning. Re-applies on no-change runs take under 30 seconds because Terraform only diffs.

issues.original: That's a complete agent stack. ALB endpoint, gateway URL, the SLS dashboard URL — paste any of them into a browser and they work.
